summary: Drew DeVault introduces scdoc, a man page generator he wrote after years of dissatisfaction with existing tools. He evaluates alternatives like asciidoc, mdocml, and ronn against three criteria: readable syntax, minimal dependencies, and man pages as a first-class target. scdoc is under 1,000 lines of C99 with zero dependencies, uses a Markdown-inspired syntax designed specifically for man pages, and weighs just 78 KiB statically linked. He has already migrated sway's documentation to scdoc.
key_points: Existing man page generators each fail at least one of three criteria: easy syntax, small size with minimal dependencies, and man pages as a first-class output target
scdoc uses a Markdown-inspired syntax that is deliberately not Markdown, because Markdown is designed for HTML, not man pages
The entire tool is under 1,000 lines of POSIX C99 with no dependencies, weighing 78 KiB statically linked against musl
Tools with runtime dependencies for reading their output (like mdocml) are considered unacceptable
money_quotes: It's inspired by Markdown, but importantly it's not actually Markdown, because Markdown is designed for HTML and not man pages.
Is less than 1,000 lines of POSIX.1 C99 code with no dependencies and weighs 78 KiB statically linked against musl libc.
My goal was to make a man page generator that had no more dependencies than man itself and would be a no-brainer for projects to use to make their manual more maintainable.
All of these are fine for the niches they fill, but not what I'm looking for. And as for GNU info… ugh.
mdocml is small and has minimal dependencies, but it has runtime dependencies - you need it installed to read the man pages it generates. This is Bad.
key_insight: A man page generator should be as minimal and dependency-free as man itself, with a syntax purpose-built for its output format rather than borrowed from HTML-oriented markup languages.
